fields.brief_block: The court held that "avoiding annoyance is never a proper basis on which to curtail protected speech," citing Coates v. City of Cincinnati, 402 U.S. 611 (1971). Bell v. Keating, 697 F.3d at 454.
fields.opinion_block: The First and Fourteenth Amendments do not permit a State to make criminal the exercise of the right of assembly simply because its exercise may be “annoying” to some people.
fields.finding_analysis:
Coates squarely supports the narrower proposition that avoiding annoyance is not a proper basis for curtailing protected speech or assembly. The Court struck down a Cincinnati ordinance criminalizing conduct “annoying to persons passing by” as both unconstitutionally vague and overbroad, holding that the First and Fourteenth Amendments do not permit a State to make criminal the exercise of the right of assembly simply because its exercise may be annoying to some people, and that mere public intolerance or animosity cannot be the basis for abridgment of these freedoms.

The substance is therefore well grounded in the cited opinion. The only gap is one of quotation form: the exact words “avoiding annoyance is never a proper basis on which to curtail protected speech” are the Seventh Circuit's formulation in Bell v. Keating, not language that appears in the Coates opinion itself. The brief's framing is accurate in attributing the underlying principle to Coates, but the quoted phrase is a reworded paraphrase rather than Coates's own text.
